data.title: Deere expects $4.5B–$5B net income in 2026 as segment guidance and order trends improve
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Deere & Company (DE) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Christopher Seibert, Manager of Investor Communications, reported that "John Deere completed the first quarter with a 5.9% operating margin for the equipment operations." Seibert highlighted that "all business segments delivered higher net sales year-over-year with both small ag and turf and Construction & Forestry top line growing by over 20%." He added, "our results for the quarter exceeded our forecast, driven by shipping volumes that were ahead of our initial plan."
  • Seibert stated, "over the course of the quarter, we saw continued strengthening of our order books across several product lines, most notably in small ag and turf as well as construction."
  • Seibert noted, "the developments over the course of the past 3 months have strengthened our belief that 2026 marks the bottom of the current cycle as we project mid-single-digit net sales growth for the equipment operations this fiscal year."
  • Joshua Jepsen, Chief Financial Officer, commented, "the first quarter demonstrated great execution from our teams. All business segments operated efficiently and delivered results ahead of plan."
  • Jepsen also noted, "over the quarter, we returned nearly $750 million in cash to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases."
  • Ryan Campbell, President of Worldwide Construction, Forestry & Power Systems, announced, "we are excited to announce our new Deere designed 20-ton class excavators at the upcoming CONEXPO show in Las Vegas... this is the first step of what will be a multiyear launch plan for a complete line of excavators."
  • Campbell addressed the digital strategy, stating, "yesterday, we completed the acquisition of Tenna... Tenna provides a leading technology platform that automates contractor workflows, gives near real-time insights into equipment operations and maintenance and enhances visibility, planning and coordination to boost productivity and cut costs."

Outlook

  • For fiscal year 2026, Seibert provided updated guidance: "our updated outlook for net income is now between $4.5 billion and $5 billion."
  • The Production & Precision Ag segment's full year forecast remains "between 11% and 13%" operating margin, with net sales still forecasted "to be down between 5% and 10%."
  • Small Ag & Turf net sales are now expected "to be up about 15%" and operating margin guidance increased to "between 13.5% and 15%."
  • Construction & Forestry 2026 net sales are now forecasted "to be up around 15%" with operating margin estimated "between 9% and 11%."
  • Projections for cash flow from equipment operations increased to "between $4.5 billion and $5.5 billion."

Financial Results

  • Net sales and revenues increased 13% to $9.611 billion, while net sales for the equipment operations rose 18% to $8.001 billion.
  • Net income attributable to Deere & Company was $656 million or $2.42 per diluted share.
  • Production & Precision Ag net sales were $3.163 billion, up 3% year-over-year, with operating profit at $139 million and a 4.4% operating margin.
  • Small Ag & Turf net sales increased 24% to $2.168 billion, with operating profit at $196 million and a 9% operating margin.
  • Construction & Forestry net sales for the quarter increased 34% year-over-year to $2.67 billion, with operating profit of $137 million and a 5.1% operating margin.
  • Worldwide Financial Services net income was $244 million in the first quarter.

Q&A

  • Kristen Owen, Oppenheimer: Inquired about pricing bridges and expectations in Production & Precision Ag (PPA) and Construction & Forestry (C&F). Josh Beal responded that incentives in South America were a factor, but "our expectation is to still maintain that 1.5 points for large ag price." Jepsen added that pricing is expected to be consistent across remaining quarters.
  • Angel Castillo Malpica, Morgan Stanley: Asked about order strength and factors driving C&F demand. Beal pointed to "contractor confidence" and backlogs, while Campbell noted broad-based activity and growing need for fleet investments.
  • Stephen Volkmann, Jefferies: Questioned the conservatism in C&F forecasts. Beal explained that prior underproduction set up C&F for a stronger upturn and positive price/currency effects.
  • David Raso, Evercore ISI: Asked about large ag order book and production rates. Beal described improved order velocity and stability in fundamentals, while Jepsen indicated "we are seeing some increased build rates in tractors, and that's kind of back half related to this order activity."
  • Timothy Thein, Raymond James: Queried price vs. cost expectations. Beal said "we're probably closer now to price/cost neutral for the full year," inclusive of tariffs.
  • Steven Fisher, UBS: Asked about regional production and margin progression. Beal noted that margin improvement is expected as North America production picks up, returning to "more normal mix going forward."
  • Charles Albert Dillard, Bernstein: Questioned tariff relief and timing. Beal said, "we won't react too quickly" to changes and would maintain a cautious, managed approach.
  • Other analysts focused on inventory management, new product launches, and market dynamics in various regions, with management emphasizing operational execution and market-driven adjustments.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism but pressed for clarity on pricing, order trends, and margin sustainability, with some skepticism about conservatism in forecasts and the sustainability of current momentum.
  • Management maintained a confident but measured tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing operational execution and market stabilization. In Q&A, the tone was generally consistent, with management providing detailed explanations and reinforcing stability: "We believe that future policies around renewable fuels and additional export opportunities should drive demand and provide continued stability."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management exhibited incrementally improved confidence, moving from a defensive stance to a more constructive outlook as order trends and segment guidance improved.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for net income increased from $4 billion–$4.75 billion previously to $4.5 billion–$5 billion.
  • Segment sales and operating margin forecasts for Small Ag & Turf and Construction & Forestry were raised, reflecting better-than-expected Q1 performance and stronger order books.
  • Management's tone shifted from prioritizing caution and inventory management to highlighting execution, stabilization, and incremental optimism.
  • Analysts' questions this quarter focused less on downside risk and more on the sustainability of positive trends, pricing bridges, and production adjustments in response to demand shifts.
  • Strategic priorities evolved from defensive production planning and cost management to seizing growth in construction and launching major new products and digital platforms.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing headwinds in South American ag equipment markets due to high interest rates and currency pressures.
  • Potential risks from tariffs remain, with projected annual tariff costs unchanged at $1.2 billion.
  • Analysts flagged potential volatility in commodity prices and uncertainties around government policy impacts and global trade flows.
  • Management emphasized ongoing cost control, inventory discipline, and flexibility to adjust production as needed in response to evolving market conditions.

Final Takeaway

Deere & Company highlighted execution ahead of plan and strengthened order trends across its core segments in the first quarter of 2026. Management increased full-year guidance for net income and raised segment sales and margin targets for Small Ag & Turf and Construction & Forestry, supported by improvements in end market demand, operational efficiency, and new product launches. While acknowledging ongoing risks in select regions and from tariffs, management signaled confidence in stabilization at the bottom of the cycle and a constructive setup for growth as the year progresses.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Global economy should grow at 3.2% sustained by AI investments and fiscal policy – Harvard economist
data.description:
Harvard economist Gita Gopinath projects the global economy will grow at approximately 3.2% in 2026, matching the previous year’s pace, buoyed by AI investment tailwinds and stimulative fiscal policy.

However, she cautioned that financial conditions appear remarkably calm despite elevated uncertainty, warning that “stressed valuations everywhere, including in the US treasury bond market, one of the core funding markets of the world… can change rapidly.”

Geopolitical tensions have emerged as a primary driver of capital flows, with Gopinath noting that when “the geopolitical distance between two countries grows to how big it’s become between the US and China, then… the amount of flows between the two countries, portfolio flows and bank flows, declined by over 15%.”

Speaking at the 2026 Joint Conference on Financial Crises hosted in Chicago by the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago and the Yale University Program on Financial Stability—in a moderated discussion with Chicago Fed President and CEO Austan Goolsbee, Gopinath advocated for a return to the pragmatic origins of international trade institutions, which recognized that countries would protect their industries while maintaining transparency through negotiated tariffs rather than pursuing pure free trade.

The dollar’s (DXY) performance has confounded economists, with Gopinath calling it “the biggest disconnect between what economists predicted for 2025 and what played out.”

Despite expectations that tariffs and expansionary fiscal policy would strengthen the dollar, it failed to recover from an 8-9% depreciation in 2025, which she attributes to increased dollar risk hedging by international institutions that previously viewed the currency as a “one-way bet.”

On digital currencies, Gopinath urged emerging market central banks to pay particular attention to stablecoins, noting that “the capital controls that exist, framework that exists, works well with the traditional finance system. It doesn’t work as well with the new kinds of fintech that exist.”

While stablecoins could improve efficiency in cross-border payments where banks earn excessive rents, she expressed skepticism about their ability to generate significant new treasury demand domestically.

Despite sharp Federal Reserve interest rate hikes and global shocks, major emerging markets have avoided crises thanks to improved domestic policies, including independent central banks and inflation-targeting frameworks.

“What it tells you is that even from a monetary sovereignty perspective, as long as you have the right policies, you can have resilience to a lot of shocks,” Gopinath said.

Looking ahead, Gopinath warned that a stock market correction could have far greater consequences than the dot-com crash, given that household exposure to equities is now twice as high relative to GDP.

She expressed concern about the lack of visibility into non-bank financial institutions, observing that “we’ve never had a crisis with such a large NBFI sector in the past.

More on the U.S. economy

  • Wholesale inventories rise 0.2% M/M in December; retail inventories flat
  • Leading indicator index edges down 0.2% in December, as expected
  • Pending home sales unexpectedly fall in January
  • Minneapolis Fed's Kashkari sees monetary policy as 'pretty close to neutral'
  • Bullish sentiment slips as neutral outlook climbs in mid-February, shows latest AAII survey

data.title: GATX expects 10% EPS growth in 2026 while integrating Wells Fargo rail acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: GATX Corporation (GATX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert Lyons highlighted that "we came into the year expecting EPS growth in the 8% range over 2024. And as reported this morning, our EPS actually increased 11% over 2024." Lyons emphasized the successful integration underway of the Wells Fargo Rail acquisition, noting its significance as the largest in GATX's history. "We put $1.3 billion of capital to work in what we believe will be attractive earnings growth and return opportunities for our shareholders."
  • Lyons detailed that GATX will consolidate 100% of the newly formed JV with Brookfield, managing a combined fleet of 208,000 railcars, and projected that "utilization for the consolidated fleet to be 98% to 99% by year-end."
  • CFO Thomas Ellman stated, "In 2026, we expect Rail North America lease revenue to be in the range of $1.6 billion or approximately $550 million over 2025," and projected segment profit at North America rail to be in the range of $415 million, up $55 million to $65 million year-over-year.
  • Lyons announced the Board's approval of an 8.2% increase in the quarterly dividend and a new $300 million share repurchase authorization, citing "the Board's confidence and the strength and quality of our cash flow, the increased scale and strength of our global businesses and the positive outlook for GATX."

Outlook

  • Lyons stated, "we expect EPS to be in the range of $9.50 to $10.10 per diluted share in 2026, which would mark another year of record EPS. Importantly, this is roughly a 10% increase in EPS in a year in which we'll complete and integrate the largest acquisition in our history."
  • Management expects Rail North America lease revenue to rise to $1.6 billion and other revenue to $160 million, with net gains on asset dispositions reaching $200 million.
  • Maintenance expense is forecast at $500 million, interest expense at $440 million, depreciation at $520 million, and other operating expense at $85 million for 2026.
  • Rail International segment profit is expected to increase by $5 million to $10 million, and Engine Leasing segment profit to increase by $15 million to $20 million in 2026.

Financial Results

  • GATX reported fourth quarter 2025 net income of $97 million or $2.66 per diluted share, compared with $76.5 million or $2.10 per diluted share in Q4 2024.
  • Full year 2025 net income reached $333.3 million or $9.12 per diluted share, up from $284.2 million or $7.78 per diluted share in 2024.
  • The company achieved ROE above 12% and invested $1.3 billion of capital during 2025.
  • At Rail North America, utilization was maintained at 99% and over $640 million of new investments were closed.
  • GATX Rail Europe acquired nearly 6,000 railcars from DD cargo and GATX India grew its portfolio to over 12,000 wagons.
  • Engine leasing saw the strongest earnings growth among business segments in 2025.

Q&A

  • Andrzej Tomczyk, Goldman Sachs: Asked about the magnitude of gains on sales and variability in business segments. Ellman answered that "we're targeting something in the range of $200 million for gains on sales" and explained that gains and maintenance spend are primary sources of variance.
  • Tomczyk: Inquired about synergies from the Wells acquisition. Lyons replied, "we gave the guidance in the press release of the $0.20 to $0.30 from the impact of the transactions that's early-stage synergies and benefits."
  • Tomczyk: Questioned engine leasing impacts from lower aircraft engine delivery. Ellman responded, "the global aviation market and aircraft engine leasing, in particular, remains very strong."
  • Ben Mohr, Citi: Asked about potential railcar shortages. Titterton replied, "there are fewer new cars being produced, and thanks to supportive scrap rates, we are seeing cars leave the fleet... we're seeing net fleet shrinkage in the North American fleet."
  • Mohr: Asked about LPI decline and renewal success rate. Lyons responded that "high teens, 20% range is very positive," and Titterton noted "commercially what we expect and consistent with history."
  • Harrison Bauer, Susquehanna: Asked about one-time costs with Wells deal. Lyons stated, "there's no significant onetime costs in there."
  • Brendan McCarthy, Sidoti: Requested CapEx breakdown. Ellman explained, "about 3/4 of that is expected to be at Rail North America and about 1/4...in Rail International."
  • Justin Bergner, Gabelli: Queried share repurchase specifics. Lyons said, "it's very open ended...we don't have a targeted amount in any given year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on variability in gains, synergies, segment performance, and capital allocation, with a tone that was neutral to slightly positive and probing for forward-looking risk.
  • Management maintained a confident and detailed tone, especially in prepared remarks, using phrases like "we expect" and "we believe" but acknowledged potential challenges in integrating the large acquisition.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more detailed and forward-looking, while analysts maintained a consistent level of scrutiny.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company raised its full-year EPS guidance from a prior range of $8.50 to $8.90 per share to a 2026 projection of $9.50 to $10.10 per share.
  • Strategic focus shifted more strongly toward integration of the Wells Fargo Rail acquisition, with expanded revenue and profit targets.
  • Key financial metrics such as ROE, asset gains, and segment profits showed upward movement.
  • Analysts continued to focus on remarketing income, maintenance costs, and integration synergies, similar to previous quarters.
  • Management's confidence increased, reflecting the momentum from the acquisition and stronger financial outlook.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted variability in gains on asset sales and maintenance spend as primary business risks for 2026.
  • Ellman cautioned that "variance in projected remarketing gains, both at Rail North America and in our engine leasing business are the biggest source of upside or downside."
  • Lyons acknowledged the integration challenges of managing a significantly larger fleet and maintaining cost efficiencies.
  • Analysts probed for risks related to supply-demand imbalances, maintenance expense volatility, and the long-term impact of the Wells Fargo acquisition.

Final Takeaway

GATX enters 2026 with record EPS guidance, supported by successful integration of the Wells Fargo Rail acquisition and robust performance across rail and engine leasing segments. The company expects to deliver double-digit EPS growth, maintain high fleet utilization, and benefit from expanded scale and diversified revenue streams, while continuing to invest in cost efficiencies and capital allocation returns for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: YETI outlines 6–8% net sales growth for 2026 while expanding global reach and product innovation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: YETI Holdings, Inc. (YETI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Matthew Reintjes emphasized a strong Q4 performance, stating, "The strong performance YETI delivered in Q4 is a direct result of growing brand strength and disciplined, consistent execution of our long-term strategy." He highlighted three key themes: a robust finish to 2025 setting the stage for global growth and profitability in 2026, an accelerating product innovation engine, and expanding global brand momentum.

  • Reintjes reported 5% net sales growth for the quarter, with Drinkware up 6% and international growth at 25%. He noted, "Gross margins exceeded expectations even in an intense tariff and promotional holiday environment, thanks to YETI's premium brand strength, innovation across the portfolio and operational execution."

  • The company executed $125 million in share repurchases during Q4, bringing the full year total to approximately $300 million.

  • Reintjes announced a leadership transition: "Mike's last day in the CFO role at YETI will be February 22...We're pleased to announce that Scott Bomar has been appointed to serve as our next CFO. Scott joins us from Home Depot, where he most recently served as SVP of Finance."

  • CFO Michael McMullen stated, "In the fourth quarter, we delivered adjusted net sales of $583.7 million, representing 5% year-over-year growth and our strongest quarterly performance of the year." He also highlighted, "Sales outside the U.S. grew 25% to $136 million, our strongest quarterly performance of the year."

Outlook

  • YETI expects full year 2026 sales to grow between 6% and 8% versus fiscal 2025, driven by innovation and expanding international markets. McMullen explained, "We expect Drinkware to grow at a mid-single-digit pace for the year, fueled by robust international demand, ongoing innovation and a continued broadening of our portfolio."

  • The company anticipates high-single-digit to low-double-digit growth in Coolers & Equipment, with international growth expected in the high teens to 20% range for the full year.

  • Gross margins are projected to be between 56% and 57% in 2026, with McMullen noting, "At the midpoint of the range, this is down approximately 90 basis points year-over-year, reflecting the annualization of a full year of tariffs."

  • Adjusted operating income margin is expected to be approximately 14.4%, with adjusted EPS guidance between $2.77 and $2.83 for 2026.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted net sales for Q4 were $583.7 million, up 5% year-over-year. Drinkware sales grew 6% to $380 million, while Coolers & Equipment sales grew 2% to $192 million.

  • Direct-to-consumer sales rose 5% to $394 million. Wholesale sales increased 6% to $189 million, with notable gains in international markets.

  • Adjusted gross profit was $341 million or 58.4% of sales. Adjusted operating income declined 14% to $94.7 million, with adjusted net income down 15% to $71.8 million. Adjusted EPS for Q4 was $0.92, down from $1.

  • The company ended Q4 with $188 million in cash and reported returning approximately $500 million to shareholders via buybacks over the past two years.

Q&A

  • Zach Beck, Baird: Asked about pricing and tariff relief. McMullen responded, "On the pricing side...it's going to be similar to what we did last year...pricing had roughly a 40 basis point impact on our gross margin, and I would expect a similar level this year." Reintjes added that any relief from tariffs is not included in current guidance.

  • Randal Konik, Jefferies: Sought details on international expansion and brand awareness. Reintjes explained the company has established teams across Europe, Japan, Asia, Canada, and Australia, with 2026 focused on building wholesale and e-commerce capabilities, plus entry into China and Korea.

  • Brooke Roach, Goldman Sachs: Asked about drivers for sequential improvement in the U.S. market. McMullen attributed it to Drinkware stabilization and growth opportunities across categories. On OpEx leverage, McMullen cited timing dynamics in brand marketing and incentive compensation, with expected leverage from facility and technology investments.

  • Phillip Blee, William Blair: Inquired about catalysts for long-term growth. Reintjes highlighted wholesale caution, domestic market monitoring, and accelerating international growth as key factors.

  • Sarah Morin, Piper Sandler: Sought insights on advertising strategy. Reintjes shared learnings from the Q4 campaign and plans to shift major campaigns to the first half of 2026, targeting high-impact moments in sports and culture.

  • Joseph Altobello, Raymond James: Asked for clarification on tariff headwinds. McMullen detailed the annualization effect, stating the majority of impact is from IEEPA tariffs.

  • Brian McNamara, Canaccord Genuity: Asked about the bags business and inventory alignment. McMullen noted bags are broader than acquired brands and remain a significant growth driver. He added, "Our inventory levels are well below where they were last year."

  • Noah Zatzkin, KeyBanc: Asked about the competitive environment. Reintjes indicated continued product portfolio expansion and constructive wholesale partner relationships.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a constructive yet cautious tone, with repeated inquiries about tariffs, international expansion, and guidance conservatism. There was a focus on how external risks and inventory trends might influence future results.

  • Management maintained confidence in prepared remarks, using phrases like "we're confident in the ability to continue unlocking the global potential of YETI." During Q&A, management addressed questions directly, often citing flexibility and discipline, and acknowledged uncertainties around tariffs with statements such as, "obviously, there's a lot of unknowns."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained focused on growth drivers and risk factors, while management's confidence persisted, supported by improved results and global momentum.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company raised its growth outlook for 2026 to 6–8% net sales growth, compared to prior guidance of 1–2% for 2025. Drinkware returned to growth in Q4, while international growth accelerated to 25%.

  • Management announced a CFO transition, with Scott Bomar joining from Home Depot, marking a significant leadership change from the previous quarter.

  • Gross margin guidance for 2026 reflects ongoing tariff pressures, but management stressed cost improvement initiatives and pricing actions to mitigate impacts.

  • Analysts continued to focus on tariffs, inventory normalization, and the sustainability of international growth, with a consistent emphasis on operational execution and risk management.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited tariffs as a meaningful margin headwind, with guidance assuming current tariff levels remain. Supply chain optimization and cost control are ongoing priorities.

  • Analysts probed the potential for tariff relief, competitive pressures, and the promotional environment. Management reiterated that no tariff relief is assumed in forecasts and highlighted flexibility in cost management.

  • Inventory levels, especially in wholesale Drinkware, were discussed as a risk, with management noting "inventory levels are down meaningfully year-over-year" and continued caution among wholesale partners.

Final Takeaway

YETI enters 2026 with momentum from a strong Q4, emphasizing innovation, international expansion, and operational discipline to drive 6–8% net sales growth. The company maintains confidence in its diversified model and product pipeline, while proactively managing tariff and supply chain risks. With new leadership and continued investment in global reach, YETI aims to sustain profitable growth and shareholder returns in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Johnson & Johnson considering sale of orthopedics business - report
data.description:
• Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) is exploring a sale of its orthopedics business, DePuy Synthes, in a deal that could have a value of at least $20B.
  • Buyout firms have already expressed interest and the healthcare giant is apparently preparing documents in advance of potential negotiations, Bloomberg reported.
  • The news service noted that several big private equity houses are looking at working together to make an offer.
  • In its Q3 2025 earnings call in October, J&J management said it was looking to spin off the orthopedics unit.  
  • DePuy Synthes had $9.3B in revenue in 2025.

data.title: CVR Energy outlines $200M–$240M capital plan for 2026 while expanding WCS processing and M&A focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CVR Energy (CVI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Mark Pytosh, President and CEO, reported consolidated net income of $90 million for the year and EBITDA of $591 million, highlighting, "At the segment level, we generated EBITDA of $411 million in the Petroleum segment, $211 million in the Fertilizer segment and a loss of $22 million in the Renewable segment." He noted that fourth quarter results were impacted by accelerated depreciation from the reversion of the Wynnewood renewable diesel unit and extended downtime at the Coffeyville fertilizer facility.
  • CEO Pytosh emphasized strategic priorities: "Our primary focus will continue to be the safe and reliable operations of our facilities...we are reevaluating our commercial optimization opportunities to drive margin capture improvement in the petroleum segment...we plan to take a more proactive approach in pursuing opportunities to expand our asset footprint."
  • Pytosh introduced new Chief Commercial Officer, Travis Capps, bringing over 30 years of refining and petrochemical leadership, stating, "We're excited to have Travis leading our commercial team as we look to better optimize our refining portfolio."
  • Dane Neumann, Executive VP and CFO, reported, "For the fourth quarter of 2025, our net loss attributable to CVR shareholders was $110 million, losses per share were $1.10, and EBITDA was $51 million." Neumann outlined key impacts, including unfavorable inventory valuation and changes in RFS liability.

Outlook

  • Neumann projected full-year 2026 consolidated capital spending of $200 million to $240 million and petroleum segment turnaround spending of $15 million to $20 million, with growth capital spending of $75 million to $90 million in 2026, "expected to be slightly elevated relative to the past few years as we hit the peak spending year for the alkylation project at Wynnewood along with a host of reliability and debottlenecking projects in the Fertilizer segment."
  • For Q1 2026, expected Petroleum segment throughput is 200,000 to 215,000 barrels per day, direct operating expenses of $110 million to $120 million, and capital spending between $30 million and $35 million. Fertilizer segment ammonia utilization rate is aimed at 95% to 100%, with capital spending between $25 million and $30 million.
  • Management cited constructive market fundamentals for refining and fertilizer, referencing, "Global refining capacity additions are set to slow down in 2026 and 2027...refined product demand growth is expected to remain steady, particularly for diesel."

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 consolidated net loss was $116 million and EBITDA was $51 million. Adjusted EBITDA was $91 million, with adjusted losses per share of $0.80.
  • Petroleum segment adjusted EBITDA was $73 million with combined throughput of approximately 218,000 barrels per day and crude utilization at 97%. Realized margin adjusted for inventory and RFS impacts was $9.92 per barrel, with a 44% capture rate on the Group 3 2-1-1 benchmark.
  • RIN prices averaged $6.05 per barrel, with net RINs expense for the quarter at $90 million. Estimated accrued RFS obligation was $72 million as of December 31.
  • Renewable segment adjusted EBITDA was breakeven for Q4, following the cessation of the renewable diesel unit at Wynnewood.
  • Fertilizer segment adjusted EBITDA was $20 million, with ammonia utilization rate at 64% due to the Coffeyville facility's delayed start-up.
  • Cash flow from operations was breakeven; free cash flow was a use of $55 million. The company ended the quarter with $511 million in cash.

Q&A

  • Manav Gupta, UBS: Asked about a "more persuasive approach to M&A" and asset expansion focus. Neumann explained, "We're looking at both sides of the business, so both our refining business and our fertilizer business...any deal that we would consider has to be accretive to our shareholders or our unitholders."
  • Gupta, UBS: On term loan paydown vs. dividend, Neumann said free cash flow and deleveraging remain priorities, noting, "We don't believe that we have to be back to our base $1 billion target before a dividend can return...we want a dividend that's going to be sustainable in any part of the cycle."
  • Matthew Blair, TPH: Inquired about ramping up WCS runs at Coffeyville. Neumann explained, "When Maduro was removed in Venezuela, that started changing the dynamics in the Western Canadian market...the best economic value of the barrel was to run it at Coffeyville rather than shipping it all down to the Gulf Coast."
  • Blair, TPH: Asked about RIN prices. Neumann said, "RIN obligation at Wynnewood is...2 to 3x what we pay everybody who works at the facility...we are trying to blend more, we're trying to take steps to reduce our overall exposure."
  • [Alexa Petrick], Goldman Sachs: Questioned capture rates and jet fuel production at Coffeyville. Pytosh said, "We're pretty early and not ready to get targets...it's really, from my perspective, a cultural shift where we are constantly looking for those margin capture opportunities."
  • [Alexa Petrick], Goldman Sachs: On product pipelines to the West Coast, Neumann stated, "I'm very optimistic about the Mid-Continent for the next several years...the upside for our company in the Mid-Con is very good."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism about M&A and dividends, with persistent focus on RIN-related costs and margin capture. The tone was probing but constructive, especially regarding growth and capital allocation strategies.
  • Management maintained a disciplined and pragmatic tone, emphasizing operational reliability, financial flexibility, and disciplined capital deployment. Pytosh and Neumann repeatedly stressed sustainable growth and cautious optimism. The language was confident but measured, with an emphasis on maintaining financial strength and flexibility.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone under new leadership was somewhat more proactive on M&A and asset optimization, while analysts remained focused on returns and operational risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for capital spending rose to $200 million to $240 million for 2026 from the prior year's range of $180 million to $200 million.
  • Strategic focus shifted under new CEO Pytosh toward more proactive asset expansion and margin optimization, including increased WCS processing at Coffeyville.
  • Analysts maintained focus on dividend reinstatement, leverage targets, and operational cost controls, echoing concerns from prior quarters.
  • Management confidence in market fundamentals and margin opportunities increased, with greater emphasis on expanding the asset footprint and commercial optimization.
  • Fertilizer segment guidance improved for Q1 2026, with ammonia utilization expected to recover above 95% following Q4 downtime.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited RIN price volatility and regulatory uncertainty as significant cost pressures, particularly for Wynnewood. Neumann highlighted, "RIN obligation at Wynnewood is our financial obligation is 2 to 3x what we pay everybody who works at the facility."
  • Downtime at the Coffeyville fertilizer facility due to third-party start-up issues and the cessation of renewable diesel operations at Wynnewood were identified as recent operational challenges.
  • Management indicated ongoing efforts to blend more or acquire blending capacity to mitigate RIN expense, while maintaining discipline in M&A and capital allocation.

Final Takeaway

CVR Energy’s fourth quarter call showcased a strategic shift under new CEO Mark Pytosh, with a sharpened focus on operational reliability, disciplined capital allocation, and proactive asset expansion. The company outlined an increased 2026 capital spending plan and plans to ramp up WCS processing at Coffeyville. Management addressed persistent RIN-related cost pressures and emphasized a commitment to sustainable dividends, while maintaining confidence in constructive market fundamentals and improved margin opportunities in both the refining and fertilizer segments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Aritzia acquires the Fred Segal brand as it accelerates its U.S. push
data.description:
Aritzia (ATZAF) (ATZ:CA) has acquired the Fred Segal brand, according to Women's Wear Daily. The deal includes the intellectual property and trademarks from Fred Segal Family LLC and  a lease on the original flagship location on Melrose Avenue in Los Angeles. Financial terms were not disclosed. 

The transaction was described by WWD as a purchase of the Fred Segal fashion and lifestyle brand rather than a deal for a broad store base. The seller had owned the core trademarks and licensed them to operators and partners, and Aritzia’s (ATZAF) (ATZ:CA) move transfers those brand rights while pairing them with control of the iconic Melrose flagship, which was damaged in a recent storm and will now be restored and repositioned. 

The broad view on the deal is that it supports Aritzia's (ATZAF) (ATZ:CA) push to accelerate its U.S. expansion and diversify beyond its namesake banner. Aritzia (ATZAF) (ATZ:CA) plans to revive and reimagine Fred Segal for a new generation by combining the brand’s heritage with its own everyday luxury retail capabilities. The Melrose site could be used as an experiential destination and lifestyle hub rather than a conventional apparel store.

data.title: Airline stocks hit as Middle East tensions drive oil to 7-month high
data.description:
Airline stocks are under pressure Thursday as escalating tensions in the Middle East push oil prices to a seven-month high, heightening concerns over fuel costs and profit margins.

As the U.S. deploys another aircraft carrier to the region amid stalled nuclear talks with Iran, the potential for a U.S. strike against Tehran as soon as this weekend pushed the price of a barrel of crude oil up another $2 on Thursday.

For airlines, the 16% increase in the price of oil from the beginning of the year will undoubtedly be reflected in Q1 results. Fuel accounts for approximately 20% to 30% of an airline’s total operating expenses and can reach as much as 40% of total expenses during extended periods of high fuel costs, making it one of the airline sectors’ largest and most volatile expenses.

While carriers can offset much of this volatility through fuel hedging, they risk locking in elevated prices if geopolitical tensions ease and oil stabilizes, potentially leading to significant losses if they misjudge the longer-term trajectory of fuel costs.

Currently, shares of American Airlines (AAL), United Airlines (UAL), and Delta Air Lines (DAL) are all down more than 5%, JetBlue (JBLU) down 9%, and Alaska Air Group (ALK) shares are down 6%. Accordingly, the U.S. Global Jets ETF (JETS) is down 4%.

data.title: Select Water upgraded at Northland on 'decisively bullish' Q4 results, commentary
data.description:
Select Water Solutions (WTTR) -2.4% in Thursday's trading despite receiving an upgrade to Outperform from Market Perform with an $18 price target, raised from $12, at Northland, citing "decisively bullish" Q4 results and commentary.

Northland analyst Bobby Brooks noted the network effect of Select Water Solutions' (WTTR) infrastructure segment is taking hold along with strong gains in the company's chemicals segment.

Brooks said he views 2026 as a catalyst-rich year for the company and thinks the free cash flow generation ability of the business will begin showing in 2027, following years of growth capital spending for infrastructure projects.

"Combine this with our street-high (and achievable) FY27 estimates, and we see a compelling story," the analyst wrote.

data.title: BrainsWay down after investment in Canadian mental care provider
data.description:
BrainsWay (BWAY) ADRs traded lower on Thursday after the Israeli MedTech announced a minority investment worth up to $2.5M in BrainStim Health, an operator of mental health clinics in Canada.

BWAY, which markets non-invasive neurostimulation treatments for psychiatric disorders, said that as part of an equity financing agreement with BrainStim, it will initially invest $1.0M in the British Columbia-based firm.

With additional milestone-based investments reaching up to $1.5B, BrainsWay (BWAY) will receive a preferred, annually compounding security in BrainStim and secure a minority position in the company. The deal allows for a redemption mechanism for investments.

“Our investment in BrainStim is now the fifth minority-stake investment into growth-oriented clinical service platforms announced by the Company,” CEO Hadar Levy said.

“Partnering with an interventional psychiatry pioneer like BrainsWay only serves to strengthen our value proposition and increase our success,” added BrainStim’s Chief Medical Officer, Venu Karapeddy.
